q: I sell a SKU for $75. My COGS is $45. What is the exact gross margin?
a:
Your exact gross margin is 40%.
Calculation:
($75.00 - $45.00) / $75.00 = 0.40 (40%)
This means for every dollar of revenue, you are keeping $0.40 to cover your operating expenses and profit.

q: I have a product priced at $200 with a 35% gross margin. What is my COGS?
a:
Your COGS (Cost of Goods Sold) is $130.
Calculation:
$200 * (1 - 0.35) = $130
This means it costs you $130 to produce or acquire the item, leaving you with $70 in gross profit.

q: My $100 product gets a 25% discount. COGS is $40. What is the new margin?
a:
Your new gross margin after the discount is 46.7%.
Here is the breakdown:

Discounted Price: $75 ($100 - 25% discount)
Profit: $35 ($75 - $40 COGS)
New Margin: ($35 / $75) = 46.67%

q: My COGS has 3 parts: materials $10, labor $5, overhead $3. What is total COGS per unit?
a:
Your total COGS per unit is $18.
Calculation:
$10 (Materials) + $5 (Labor) + $3 (Overhead) = $18

q: Fixed costs are $10,000/month. Contribution margin is $20/unit. How many units to break even?
a:
You need to sell 500 units per month to break even.
Calculation:
$10,000 (Fixed Costs) / $20 (Contribution Margin per Unit) = 500 units
Every unit sold after the 500th will contribute $20 directly to your net profit.

q: How much revenue do I need to achieve $50,000 profit at a 25% net margin?
a:
To achieve a $50,000 profit at a 25% net margin, you need $200,000 in total revenue.
Calculation:
$50,000 / 0.25 = $200,000
Achieving a 25% net margin is an excellent goal, but it requires very healthy gross margins at the product level to cover your marketing, shipping, and operational costs.

q: Three SKUs with margins 40%, 30%, 20% sell 100, 200, 300 units respectively. What is the weighted av
a:
Your weighted average margin is 26.67%.
Calculation:

SKU 1: 40% × 100 = 4,000
SKU 2: 30% × 200 = 6,000
SKU 3: 20% × 300 = 6,000
Total: (4,000 + 6,000 + 6,000) / 600 units = 26.67%

This demonstrates how your high-volume, low-margin products (SKU 3) pull down your overall store profitability.

q: My COGS is $40. If costs increase by 15%, what is the new COGS and margin at $100 price?
a:
If your costs increase by 15%, your new COGS will be $46.00 and your gross margin will drop to 54%.
The Breakdown:

New COGS: $40.00 * 1.15 = $46.00
Gross Profit: $100.00 - $46.00 = $54.00
New Margin: ($54.00 / $100.00) = 54%
